date: 2026-07-06
video_id: 72jLL466p1M
url: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=72jLL466p1M
title: How Much Runway Does This Bull Market Have Left?

kicking off a new week with the big question. How much runway does this bull market have left? Darius Dale is founder and CEO of 42 Macro and joins us now. Darius, great to have you on. >> It's a real pleasure to be here. Thanks for having me, Caroline, and happy 250th to you and your family. >> Thank you so much and to you. So, Darius, we have the Dow sitting near an all-time high, the S&P 500 not too far from it, and a lot of investors wondering whether they're too late to this game. based on your macro work, are we still early in this bull market or are we getting closer to the later innings? >> Well, that's an excellent question. I love that you posed the sort of inning question because I think we're a lot closer to the seventh inning stretch than I think the median investor realizes uh for a couple of reasons. One, we think the markets will eventually have to uh transition to price in peak or transition from pricing in uh peaking inflation to ultimately sticky inflation. And we think that transition is ahead of us over the next 1 to two quarters. And then secondarily, you're getting a lot of signals from the market itself to the Mac 7 companies to a lot of the AI capex spenders to perhaps throttle down on the rate of change on the acceleration of all that capex. And if they do that, then it's likely to have a negative impact. Uh it's going to ripple through financial markets negatively in terms of revising down sales and earnings growth expectations. So that's kind of a one-two punch that we see as developing market risk over the next call it one to two quarters. But ultimately we don't think this is the ninth inning. We don't think the game is over. We think the 42 macro paradigm seull market is legs into and perhaps even through 2027. >> Okay. But let's talk about what pricing in sticky inflation could mean for where this market goes in the next one to two quarters. >> Yeah. Look, I mean, we've been coaching our global investor community to anticipate something like a summer of 1998 style market dynamic. You know, we don't want to be too specific on, you know, the the the magnitude and and the timing because in our opinion, we think market timing is for newsletter writers. You know, we are serious institutional investors here at 42 Macro in terms of trying to keep our clients on the right side of market risk. But just kind of looking at that, you know, 1998 example, we saw that the S&P 500 rallied about 20% in the first half of 1998. We were obviously in the, you know, full full throttle full throttle tech bubble uh uh back then. But then ultimately, uh, as we saw some of the issues develop with Russia, the default and long-term capital management, we ultimately saw the market pull back about 20%. We eviscerated the entire up 20 plus% year-to-ate return, but then ultimately the Fed started cutting interest rates and easing monetary policy uh to offset some of those macro dynamics. And then ultimately we had a very very vicious rally off those lows in August into into year end. And so we ultimately finished up plus 29% uh in 1998, but this is inclusive of a 20% rally, a 20% crash, and a vicious Vbottom after that. So we're expecting something similar. >> Okay. So help us wrap our heads around that. We're up about 10% year to date in the S&P 500, only about 1% off all-time highs. So you're expecting a a correction before year end, but then ultimately you think we'll end uh this year higher. >> Yeah, ultimately. So what we've been we've been Exactly. And that's a great way to characterize it. You know, we've been essentially saying the Fed needs to play action pass to set up the run. And if you think about what the what the passing does is it allow, you know, regaining a little bit of credibility on inflation fighting would go a long way for the Kevin Walsh Fed to allow for this, you know, this AI capex buildout to continue in a way that doesn't drain resources and tax the productive other sectors of the economy in a way that pushes up overall the general price level in the economy. And so they ultimately need to regain a little bit of credibility on inflation fighting. What we've been saying is back the safeties and the linebackers up. That's the bond market. Essentially get the bond market on board with, you know, this is a Federal Reserve that is serious about its 2% inflation target that's serious about its price stability mandate. But ultimately, where we think this is all headed, and this is why we don't believe the bull market is over, is when you look at the five task forces that Kevin Walsh laid out at the June FOMC, the communications task force, the balance sheet task force, the data and and and the data task force, the productivity and jobs task force, and the inflation task force. The net result of those five task forces, in our opinion, based on our deep understanding of the data and all the critical variables and how they connect together, in our opinion, we think the net result of those task forces will be very doubbish on a net basis. Not every single one of them, we don't think the balance sheet is going to be positive. We don't think the communications task force will be positive, but the net result is likely to lead to a much more doubbish monetary policy and a doubbish reaction function starting in like late 2026 or maybe even early 2027. And if that is true, you can't go from peak inflation to sticky inflation to dovish monetary policy. They're going to have to have some sort of intermission there that says that sends a message to the bond market that they're serious about inflation. >> Okay. So, the the market needs to price in this sticky inflation. Do you think the market also needs to price in rate hikes then or no? Well, we've been of the view that rate hikes are the wrong tool to address the current drivers of inflation coming out of the economy. We know that the bottom of the K, whether it be the consumer sector, whether it be the uh the business sector, and ultimately whether it be just generally interest rates sensitive sectors, the bottom of the K in the economy, if we're being honest here, is still in recession. uh when you look at you know real consumer durable goods growth when you look at residential fixed investment growth you look at non-residential fixed investment growth in structures in particular uh you look at you know delinquency rates for credit cards auto loans student loans those are essentially at or near levels that are consistent with the height of the global financial crisis right now on an aggregated basis delinquency rates are consistent with you know where we were at the trough of the great recession and so that tells you that there's very clearly a a a a downturn going on for the households and the businesses on the lower end of the K-shaped economy. So using the policy rate tool in our opinion would be a mistake because ult all all you're going to do is exacerbate the downturn that we're seeing in that segment of the economy without actually addressing where the inflation is coming from because obviously if they're in recession they're not the contributors to the inflation. Where inflation is coming from right now is three main vectors. One, we obviously have a massive aggregate demand shock from AI capex. When you look at the roughly $800 billion of of hyperscala capex that's expected for 2026 that's about 2 and a.5% of US nominal GDP. The $1.2 trillion that's currently projected for 2027 is about 3.6% of nominal GDP. I mean this is this is nuts. I mean I don't have another word to say this. This is insane how big the massive accurate demand shock this is from the perspective of of the economy. So that's one. The other two vectors are policy driven and monetary driven. On the policy side of things, we have, you know, growth and and deficit spending tracking about, you know, eight nine percent year-over-year. We have the Fed's rate of change, the annualized rate of change, the Fed's monetary uh their monetization of US public debt because, again, they're still doing reserve management purchases. That's somewhere around, you know, 6 or 7% uh year-over-year. And then you have bank credit growth growing at about 7 to 8% year-over-year. These numbers are incredibly inconsistent with 2% inflation. And so ultimately we know that all that's kind of really going to and being driven by the top part of the K-shaped economy. And so the Fed in our opinion would be do a much better job of targeting this current level of inflation, this current inflation pressures by using its balance sheet first by ending reserve management purchases, but ultimately reinstating quantitative tightening uh and threatening to do even more of that uh heading into the fall into next year, which will ultimately in our opinion give the bond market some comfort that this is a Federal Reserve that is serious about inflation. yet you're bullish so you aren't necessarily painting the picture of you know a bullish landscape especially since in addition to that you said companies might start throttling down I think you said on capex so give us the reasons that you think that this is a market that can continue to run higher >> yeah well we don't think ultimately the federal reserve is going to end the AI capex bubble uh so that's unlikely we if we the mark in our opinion we think the market would ultimately reward some discipline by the the capital spenders uh the the the capex spenders because ultimately what we're seeing from the end user data is that they're you know they're no longer burning tokens you know like it's going out of style. You know they're the the companies that they're selling the the compute to uh in the real economy are actually throttling back their usage because they're not seeing the the advent of productivity growth as quickly as as as as you know the capex spenders would probably like. And so ultimately we think the market will reward that discipline because it'll ultimately elongate the process. As long as you elongate the process and don't end the process then you still have an upward sloping earnings curve which albeit maybe having to get revised down a little bit from a rate of change perspective but ultimately it'll settle out in a still you know uptrending you know expectation from the perspective of earnings and sales growth. So you know the the the micro side of the story isn't over. We know we're going to be, you know, spending eight to eight perhaps, you know, even higher than a trillion dollars in in capex between now uh and 2030. We know [clears throat] there's a lot of money that needs to go into the ground and from picks and troubles perspective. So this this trade this this AI buildout is not over. And more importantly, as we said earlier, the net result of the five task forces, you know, if we think we're right, I obviously we think we're right on this. If we're right on that view that it's going to be doubbish, then you have the markets are going to have to price in a significant amount of right to tail risk starting, you know, in our opinion, our current expectation of when they outline the the findings of the task forces is in the December FOMC. And so if they outline a a very dovish monetary policy outlook for 2027 at the December FOMC, then the market's going to have to price in a significant amount of right tail risk. And that's how you have a significant upcycle uh in 2027. And I think the markets will bottom well ahead of that because the markets are forward-looking. >> Okay. So we saw chipmakers struggle last week. Do you look at that then as a healthy reset or a crack in the AI trade? And if leadership broadens from here, where should investors be looking next? >> Yeah, it's a great question. So it's it's a healthy reset. It's an appropriate crack. It should be cracking. I mean again we we were we the the the expectation heading into last quarter was that AI capex was an asmtote that was just going to keep going higher and in our opinion we think that that that that is no longer true that at least according to the median investor that is no longer true. So ultimately the the investors have to migrate to a to to a to understanding that you know trees don't grow to the moon. We're not going to be able to just ratchet up AI capex indefinitely because ultimately we're not, you know, the cost of all this compute from the perspective of the businesses that are using the service using the AI uh the cost is not yet justified by the uh the expansion and productivity growth and we think it ultimately will be uh justified but you know that there's a mismatch there's a timing mismatch that needed to be reset and so we're in our opinion we're in that reset process but ultimately in our opinion where we think this is headed longer term and this in our opinion we think this trade has a lot of legs from a from from a time from an X-axis standpoint is our source of funds thing. We've been of the view since last fall that investors should use the MAG7 companies to capitalize as a source of funds to capitalize other businesses particularly other businesses down in cap and out across geographic borders. Uh you know you think about the you know midcaps for instance here in the US or or or international stocks by and large they've been left for dead from a valuation perspective. But what ultimately is going to happen is AI, this technology is going to diffuse across cap. It's going to diffuse across borders. And it's ultimately going to cause a compression in the relative spread between Mac 7 companies productivity, margins, and earnings with everyone else. And that compression in our opinion is likely to lead to a compression in valuation between the Mac 7 and all the other businesses and companies. And so we've been instructing our global uh uh global investor community quite successfully since last fall to use the MAG7 as a source of funds to capitalize everything else. And as we can see everything else is demonstrabably outperforming. >> And just to clarify using the MAG 7 as a source of funds means trimming profits from the MAG7 and buying basically the the S&P equal weight index. >> S&P equal weight midcaps uh is a great segment of the market to be focused on. uh international equities is a great segment of the market to be focused on. I mean there's been a significant compression in the relative productivity the the margins as well as the the earnings expectations between US markets and international markets you know cap weighted US markets relative to uh uncap weighted markets we've seen the compression in the operating metrics already and in our opinion we believe this technology will you know perpetuate that compression and perpetuate the the you know the the expand you know the the the improvement in in operating metrics for some of these companies that have been left for dead from a valuation perspective But what it what has not happened yet to a in our opinion to a to a significant enough degree is the compression in valuation. In our opinion, we think that compression and valuation has has legs from from the perspective of the next, you know, at least one to two years, perhaps even much longer than that. >> But do you still want exposure to the mag 7 at all? And what about those investors who maybe aren't mag seven heavy, but they're S&P 500 heavy? What do they do? >> Yeah. What what in our opinion you so we would not we want max 7 exposure at all right now because again you're they're eviscerating their own free cash flow. Now if they finally get some discipline if the market gives them discipline if they get discipline from the market to slot to slow down that rate then maybe they become a an attractive buy from a valuation perspective but again just as an investor you want to skate to where the puck is going not to where the skate the puck has been. And so what we're essentially telling you and have been telling our global investor community since the last fall is the puck is going elsewhere. The puck, you want to go chase the new earnings growth. Go find the new free cash flow growth. Go find the expansion in gross margins. And it's not it's not Mac 7. It's other companies. It's the companies that are going to adopt AI to increase their productivity. It's the companies that are going to adopt AI to increase their profitability. It's the companies that going to adopt AI to increase their their earnings expectations. And we've seen this movie before. You know, we saw it in in the equities market going back to early 2000 when China joined the WTO in 2001. Uh, you know, they gave it China access to a unified global market much like, you know, AI is this unified technology that that creates productivity for most businesses, at least, you know, service industry businesses. And so, we saw China outperform US markets by about 500 percentage points from the time they joined the WTO to the middle of the 2000s. And that's exactly what this the kind of you know that's the kind of technology transfer open market access that we're essentially calling for for you know going down in cap here in the US and and and and across our geographic borders to to international stocks. We saw this movie before in Europe as well. You go back to uh the Mastrix treaty which was signed in 1992 which was the creation of the euro. You know, from the time the treaty was signed in 1992 to the time the euro was officially adopted and and introduced in 1999, we saw a massive compression in credit spreads across all the peripheral countries in Europe. For example, Italian BTPs, they were trading about 600 700 basis points above treasuries, whereas German booms are about, you know, on par with treasuries. We saw that entire 6700 basis point spread compress to where the German boom was because of the adoption of the single monetary policy, the common monetary policy, the common currency. And so we're essentially saying AI equals euro AI equals you know uh open access to to the international markets much like in China's case. And so if that is true in terms of this source of funds seeing this convergence expectation that's going to last for years. >> Okay. So for those investors who are heavily exposed to the S&P 500, they need to be making some changes as well. >> Yeah. In our opinion, yes, absolutely. Yeah. We don't again this trade has legs. you the the the the valuation alone will tell you where investors are crowded in terms of their positioning. Investors are crowded in all these names that have worked for many years and and appropriately so. This is where the cash flow growth was. This is where the gross margins were. And we're essentially saying that on a relative basis, the cash flow growth and the gross margin expansion will not come from these companies. They will come from other companies going down in cap midcaps in the US and go international equities abroad. And you don't have to be specific a sector. You don't have to pick stocks in this in this trade. This is a long-term structural macro tailwind if you're in the right, you know, exposures, you know, going down in cap, equal weight, S&P, international. This is a long-term macro headwind. If you're stuck being, you know, allocated to things that, you know, everyone that that if you stuck to yesterday's trade, if you're if you're stuck allocated to yesterday's winners, then your portfolio is going to perform like you're stuck allocated to yesterday's winners. And that's what we're trying to coach our investor community to to to to offset. And then just quickly, if you still want exposure to the AI trade, then how do you get that if you aren't getting it through the MAG7? >> If you want exposure to the AI trade, so obviously the picks and shovels have been working. The semiconductors trade was working. We're in a risk on reflation market regime. And that's exactly what you would expect. High beta growth technology. These are the types of things that work in risk on market regimes, which our market regime now casting process has signaled we we've been in since April 11th. And so, you know, since April 11th, our our our global investor community has been going further out on the risk spectrum, chasing higher beta equities, more cyclical exposures in the equity market, you know, going down in and going further out on the risk spectrum in in the credit markets. And that's exactly the kind of behavior you want to take as an investor to maximize upside capture uh in a riskon uh reflation market regime. We're essentially arguing, going back to the beginning of our conversation, Caroline, is that we're essentially saying we might be nearing the end of this reflation market regime. I'm not, you know, we're not going to be specific on timing because that that's, you know, again, we don't we don't market time. We don't predict. We're just, you know, we're fast reactors. We're going we're essentially saying the distribution of probable economic policy market outcomes is likely to evolve. It has been evolving and it's likely to continue evolving in a negative manner. And if it evolves in a negative enough manner, then we're going to have a massive disconnect between being in a risk-on market regime and having a lot of left tail risk to price in. And so right now that spread that that that that that awkward you know kind of you know disconnect has gotten increasingly awkward in recent weeks. And so at some point in the next one and two quarters we're expecting it to be so awkward that the markets actually have to price in a riskoff market regime that looks something like summer of 1998. And so that that in our opinion is a reasonable probability outcome. And if that's true, then you have to be, you know, just passionately execute any risk management process that tells you to, you know, take down risk to come in on the credit spectrum to go up in cap to, you know, to to to to take a little bit of risk off the table, uh, to give yourself a better opportunity to buy what we ultimately think will be a very viable dip at some point this summer, this fall. >> Okay. And and just digging into that a bit more, it has to be quick because we're just about out of time, but should it if we do see that play out, if we do see kind of this pullback that you're expecting within still a bull market that can charge higher, how should investors actually respond? Should they buy any dips? Should they take some profits or should they just stay the course? >> In our opinion, we think when our system says, you know, it's time to to head for the exits. It's the riskoff marketers. You need to be reigning in risk. You need to be coming in on the credit spectrum. you need to be going up in cap. You need to be taking down the beta of your overall portfolio, maybe even raising a bit of cash to the extent that, you know, that's what our kiss and doctor most signals are are signaling. But ultimately, what we're essentially coaching our invest community to saying is that that is likely to be a transitory outcome. If that outcome occurs, then it's likely to be a transitory outcome. In our opinion, we think would have caused the outcome uh to occur is the Federal Reserve trying to, you know, create a little bit of more more credibility, a little bit of scope to to ease monetary policy more significantly later in the year by tightening more than what's currently priced in at the current juncture. And if that doesn't happen, then it's unlikely we have a material enough correction to warrant taking down risk. And if that does occur, we're going to continue to rally and we're going to have an inflation problem in 2027. >> Okay. So, if the Fed decides to hike is when you might change your bullish tone. >> Am I getting right? No. >> Yeah. No. We we run very sophisticated quantitative algorithms to tell us, you know, what to do in financial markets. you know, none of the none of our fundamental views have any impact on my or my investor our investors portfolio. So, you know what I'm essentially saying I'm I'm predicting the predictors, if you will. I'm saying I think the algorithms are going to tell us to take down risk in the future because of these fundamental dynamics. If we're wrong on those fundamental dynamics and the algorithms don't tell us to take down risk, then we're going to continue to bubble and ultimately have an inflation problem in 2027. >> Okay. All right. I think this is a great time to transition to our rapid fire game of this or that. Quick questions, quick answers. Are you ready, Darius? >> I love it. This is awesome. I love this segment. >> All right, here we go. Second half. Risk on or risk off? >> Both. [laughter] >> No hedging. >> Both. [laughter] Risk on first half of second half. Risk Oh, sorry. Risk off first half of the second half. Risk on second half of the second half. >> Stocks or bonds? >> Stocks. Individual stocks or ETFs? >> ETFs. >> US or international? >> International. >> Large caps or small caps? >> Middle caps. Midcaps. >> Equal weight or market cap weight? >> Equal weight. >> Mag 7 or the rest of the market? >> Definitely not mag 7. Rest of the market. >> No surprise there. Healthy chip reset or AI cracking? Healthy chip reset. AI is not cracking. >> AI spending boom. Are we in the middle of it or near the end? >> Middle. >> Broadening market or AI still leads? >> Broadening market, but AI probably anything tethered to AI will continue to lead in our opinion. That's what the earnings growth is. >> One sector that's just getting started. >> Oo, I love this. one sector that's just getting everyone keeps trying to buy healthcare. I don't have a real strong view on that uh in our opinion, but if we go into a riskoff market regime, investors are going to want, you know, more defensive uh lower beta plays. And so healthcare is a sector that could potentially uh pick up some relative low flows in that in that scenario. >> One sector that's fairly valued here, >> a sector that's fairly valued here, technology. Next 12 months, buy the dip mentality or play it safe? >> Buy the dip. >> Economy resilient or overheating? >> We invented the resilient US economy theme in the summer of 2022 and we see no change to to that view. >> Inflation cooling or sticking around? >> Sticking around. That's the biggest that's the biggest misnomer in markets right now. Markets are focused on peak inflation appropriately. So we think one to two quarters from now they'll be very much focused on sticky inflation and and the lack of progress towards 2%. Sticky inflation at what level? >> Close to three. >> Higher for longer rates or rate cuts. >> Uh they will be cutting rates a lot in 2027 if we're right on the task forces. >> Recession in 2027. Yes or no? >> Oh no. God, no. [laughter] We're economy is going to go 3% just from AI capex alone. Absolutely not. No. One word to describe how you're feeling about the market for the rest of this year. >> Senguin >> Darius Dale, founder and CEO of 42 Macro. Thanks so much for playing along. We really appreciate your insights as well. >> Appreciate you, Caroline. Thank you for having me. I look forward to next time.
